content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission

P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662        322-5660         322-5901              322-6441              322-6444

February 22, 1983


Mrs. Barbara Jenke
Arthur Griffin, M.D.
C. Ray Moore
Members of the Board of Directors
Westside Hospital District
c/o James Jenke, M.D., Inc.
109 Adkisson Way
Taft, CA 93268

Re: Your Mutual Requests for
     Written Advice, Our File
     No. A-83-001

Dear Directors:

     Each of you has separately requested that we provide written
advice to you regarding your obligations under the Political
Form Act/ (the "Act"). Because the facts of your situa-
tions are so intricately intertwined, we have chosen to respond
to you in one, combined advice letter. The facts are complex
and will be stated below.

FACTS

     Each of you was recently elected to the West Side Hospital
District Board of Directors. Each of you commenced your
respective term of office on Janauary 6, 1983. The West Side
Hospital District ("West Side") is a public entity and a local
governmental agency within the meaning of the Act. It is
organized pursuant to Division 23 of the Health and Safety Code
(commencing with Section 32000) known as "The Local Hospital
District Law". The Board of Directors is publicly elected
pursuant to Health and Safety Code Section 32100.

_________________________

1/ Government Code Sections 81001-91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise
specified. Our advice is limited solely to issues raised under
the Act. Questions involving other provisions of law, such as
Section 1090, should be addressed to other counsel, such as that
for the West Side Hospital District.

Jenke, et al.
February 22, 1983
Page 2


Mrs. Barbara Jenke

Mrs. Jenke is a director of West Side. She is the spouse of
James Jenke, M.D., who has a medical practice in Taft. She
serves as Dr. Jenke's office manager. Dr. Jenke's practice is
incorporated (James Jenke, M.D., Inc.) and Mrs. Jenke is an
officer in that business entity (Secretary-Treasurer).
Dr. Jenke is the sole shareholder in the corporation.2/ He
draws a salary as does Mrs. Jenke. Profits are distributed to
Dr. Jenke through an "end-of-year bonus". Mrs. Jenke has a
community property interest in one-half of Dr. Jenke's earnings.

Dr. Jenke's practice ("Jenke, Inc.") receives income from
West Side's Nursing Home in the sum of $750 per month as its
Medical Director. In addition, Dr. Jenke receives income from
the patients for seeing them at the Nursing Home. Dr. Jenke
also is referred patients by the West Side District Hospital
("Hospital") for interpretations of various tests, including
EKGs.3/ Dr. Jenke also currently serves, without compensa-
tion, as both Chief of Medical Staff for the Hospital and as the
Medical Director for the Hospital's Cardiopulmonary Laboratory.

Jenke, Inc. rents its medical office space in the Medical
Arts Building from Dr. Arthur Griffin, M.D., Inc. Dr. Griffin
is also a member of the West Side Board of Directors. Jenke,
Inc. pays Dr. Griffin well in excess of $250 per month in rent
for this space and use of the x-ray facilities. This payment is
a flat rate and Jenke, Inc. then bills its patients for the
x-ray services.

Arthur Griffin, M.D.

Dr. Griffin is a Director of West Side. He is a practicing
physician and his practice is also incorporated. Dr. Griffin is
a member of the Active Medical Staff at the Hospital.
Dr. Griffin sees patients at the Hospital and receives income
from the patients for these services in excess of $250 per

2/ Mrs. Jenke has advised that state law provides that
only a licensed M.D. can be a shareholder in a medical
professional corporation. (See Corporations Code Sections 13401
and 13403.)

3/ The Hospital bills the patients for Dr. Jenke's
services and he is paid by the Hospital according to an agreed
rate per test interpretation.

Jenke, et al.
February 22, 1983
Page 3


month. Occasionally, a patient is referred to him by the
Hospital.

Dr. Griffin is a co-owner4/ of the Medical Arts Building,
along with another doctor, Dr. V. Tangpraphaphorn. Jenke, Inc.
pays rent to Dr. Griffin. Dr. Griffin and Dr. Tangpraphaphorn
owned and operated a medical laboratory (Taft Medical Center
Laboratory) which is now leased to C. Ray Moore and his spouse
who operate it as their business, paying rental to Drs. Griffin
and Tangpraphaphorn. In addition, Dr. Griffin receives "no
charge" lab services from the Moore's, up to a certain maximum
number per month, beyond this maximum, Dr. Griffin pays for
certain services. In return, Dr. Griffin serves as the Medical
Director for the laboratory and holds its operating license, for
which he receives no separate payments.

In addition, Dr. Griffin was denied Hospital staff anesthesia
privileges in 1981. He believes that this denial, by the
Hospital Administrator, which was upheld by the West Side
District Board (of which none of you were there a member)
constituted a denial of due process and violated his rights.
Dr. Griffin is contemplating legal action against West Side and
the former administrator for damages resulting from this denial
of staff anesthesia privileges.

C. Ray Moore

Mr. Moore is a director of West Side. He is a licensed
Medical Technologist, as is his wife. Together they operate the
Taft Medical Center Laboratory ("TMCL"). They pay rent to
Drs. Griffin and Tangpraphaphorn for both space in the Medical
Arts Building and various lab equipment. Dr. Griffin serves as
TMCL's Medical Director, and he receives approximately $1,400
worth of "no charge"5/ laboratory services annually, in

_______________________
4/ Dr. Griffin owns one portion of the building where he
has his offices and leases out some of this space to Jenke,
Inc. However, he has deeded his share of the office building to
a trust for his children. Dr. Griffin is the sole shareholder
in Dr. Arthur Griffin, M.D., Inc.

5/ These services involve lab work for physical exams
performed by Dr. Griffin under contract to local employers.
Neither Dr. Griffin, the patient, nor the employer is billed by
TMCL for these services; however, Dr. Griffin receives payment
for these services under the contract with the employer. If his
need for these services exceeds $350 per calendar quarter, then
he pays the lab for its work.

Jenke, et al.
February 22, 1983
Page 4


addition to which he pays TMCL for services in excess of that
level. These payments may well exceed $500 annually. State law
requires TMCL to have a pathologist as its medical director. If
Dr. Griffin did not serve in this capacity, then TMCL would have
to pay another doctor to provide this service.

Generally, area physicians refer patients to TMCL for
laboratory work. A patient cannot have laboratory work
performed unless a physician has prescribed that work. Most
work performed by TMCL is billed directly to the patient (or
insurance carrier where applicable), not to the physicians;
however, some work is billed to the physician. Jenke, Inc. and
Dr. Griffin both regularly refer clients to TMCL for lab work.

The Hospital also has a lab of its own, which performs some
services in competition with TMCL. Mr. Moore has received some
information to the effect that some doctors are required by the
Hospital to use its lab rather than TMCL. Mr. Moore would like
to have the Hospital lab refer tests to his lab (TMCL) which are
currently being referred to labs outside the community. TMCL
performs these tests which no other lab in the community
performs.

QUESTION

Each of you has asked for advice as to your respective
duties regarding possible disqualification under the Act in
light of the foregoing facts. In particular, the West Side
Board will be taking the following actions at its next meeting,
February 23, 1983:

1. The Board will review and approve or disapprove checks
drawn to pay Dr. Jenke for his services as Medical Director at
West Side's Nursing Home and his interpretation work at the
Hospital. This is normally a routine matter and these checks
have never been disapproved.

2. The Board will consider the extension of various staff
privileges to both Dr. Jenke and Dr. Griffin.

ANALYSIS

The analysis will consider each of you, in turn, both
generally and with respect to the two specific issues coming up
on February 23rd. First, the general principals of the Act
regarding conflicts of interest will be set forth.

Jenke, et al.
February 22, 1983
Page 5


The Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or using his or her official position to
influence the making of, a decision in which he or she knows or
has reason to know that he or she has a financial interest.
Section 87100. A financial interest is defined in Section 87103
as follows:

An official has a financial interest in a decision
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on
the public generally, on:

(a) Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
more than one thousand dollars ($1,000).

(b) Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
more than one thousand dollars ($1,000).

(c) Any source of income, other than loans
by a commercial lending institution in the regular
course of business on terms available to the
public without regard to official status, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in
value provided to, received by or promised to the
public official within 12 months prior to the time
when the decision is made.

(d) Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.

For purposes of this section, indirect investment
or interest means any investment or interest owned by
the spouse or dependent child of a public official, by
an agent on behalf of a public official, or by a
business entity or trust in which the official, the
official's agents, spouse, and dependent children own
directly, or beneficially a 10 percent interest or
greater.

With these basic principles in mind we turn now to their
application to the facts at hand.

Jenke, et al.
February 22, 1983
Page 6


                              Mrs. Barbara Jenke

     Mrs. Jenke has a financial interest in Jenke, Inc., which is
a source of income to her of more than $250 (Section 87103(c)).
She also has an investment of more than $1,000 in Jenke, Inc.
(Section 87103(a)) and serves as both an officer and an employee
of Jenke, Inc. (Section 87103(d)).


     Jenke, Inc. receives money from West Side. We must
determine whether this money constitutes income within the
meaning of the Act in order to know whether West Side may be
considered a source of income to Mrs. Jenke, which could require
her to disqualify herself as to most decisions affecting West
Side.

     Income is defined in Section 82030(a) as follows:

          (a)  "Income" means, except as provided in sub-
     section (b), a payment received, including but not
     limited to any salary, wage, advance, dividend,
     interest, rent proceeds from any sale, gift, including
     any gift of food or beverage, loan, forgiveness or
     payment of indebtedness received by the filer,
     reimbursement for expenses, per diem, or contribution
     to an insurance or pension program paid by any person
     other than an employer, and including any community
     property interest in income of a spouse. Income also
     includes an outstanding loan. Income of an individual
     also includes a pro rata share of any income of any
     business entity or trust in which the individual or
     spouse owns, directly, indirectly or beneficially, a
     10-percent interest or greater. "Income," other than a
     gift, does not include income received from any source
     outside the jurisdiction and not doing business within
     the jurisdiction, not planning to do business within
     the jurisdiction, or not having done business within
     the jurisdiction during the two years prior to the time
     any statement or other action is required under this
     title.

     Section 82030(b) excludes certain things from the definition of
income which would otherwise be covered by Section 82030(a). In
pertinent part, Section 82030(b) reads as follows:

          (b)  "Income" also does not include:

                              * * *

Jenke, et al.
February 22, 1983
Page 7


     (2)  Salary and reimbursement for expenses or
per diem received from a state, local, or federal
government agency and reimbursement for travel
expenses and per diem received from a bona fide
educational, academic or charitable organization.

The specific question which we must address is whether the money
received by Jenke, Inc. as a result of Dr. Jenke's various
services rendered to and at the Hospital and Nursing Home is
salary. We determine that it is salary. A Hospital cannot
actually perform medical services, but must do so through its
licensed physicians. (See, Advice Letter to Ronald Kaldor, No.
17I-244, copy enclosed.) The Hospital could employ its own
full-time staff or arrange for physician services on a part-time
or as-needed basis. In the case of a small hospital, the latter
arrangement takes more sense as it permits greater diversity of
services. Consequently, under the circumstances given here, we
consider the payments which Jenke, Inc. receives for his work as
Medical Director of the Nursing Home and for interpretive work
at the Hospital to be salary from a local government entity,
subject to the exclusion under subdivision (b)(2) of Section
82030.

As to the income which Jenke, Inc. receives from the
patients whom he treats at either the Nursing Home or the
Hospital, we conclude that neither the Hospital nor the Nursing
Home is a source of that income. This conclusion is different
from that reached in the Kaldor letter (supra) because in Kaldor
the physician's income was almost entirely dependent upon the
contract with the hospital.5/ In Kaldor, the doctor was, in
essence, an employee of the hospital, while for Jenke, Inc.
there is no evidence that this is the case. Dr. Jenke has an
ongoing practice separate and distinct from the Hospital; he has
patients who utilize his services totally distinct from the
Hospital; and his relationship with the Hospital does not
involve an exclusive contract to service the patients of the
Hospital (with the exception of the interpretive work previously
discussed, which is a salary).


     5/ "Virtually all of the physician's medical income is
received from the services rendered to hospital patients under
the contract with the [physician's] corporation. Although the
physician is able to bring in patients for services, who are not
patients of the hospital, this is not usually done.

Jenke, et al.
February 22, 1983
Page 8


       Therefore, neither the Nursing Home nor the Hospital is a
source of income to Jenke, Inc. (and hence Mrs. Jenke) within
the meaning of Section 87103. Consequently, Mrs. Jenke is not
disqualified from participating in decisions which will have a
material financial effect on West Side.

       We have previously held that an official is not disqualified
from participating in a decision involving the governmental
salary of his or her spouse.7/ The exemption in Section
82030(b)(2) permits an official to make decisions on his or her
own government salary. Similarly, an official can make deci-
sions concerning a government salary in which he has a community
property interest. Consequently, Mrs. Jenke may participate in
the decisions involving approval of the checks for Dr. Jenke's
work at the Hospital and the Nursing Home, as well as in
decisions involving Dr. Jenke's rate of compensation for these
duties.8/

       However, decisions which do not involve Dr. Jenke's salary
received from West Side, but which do involve a material finan-
cial effect on Jenke, Inc. or on Mrs. Jenke through Jenke, Inc.
(see, 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702, copy enclosed), will
necessitate disqualification by Mrs. Jenke. For example, a
decision involving the extension, expansion or restriction of
Dr. Jenke's privileges at the Hospital would likely have such an
effect upon Jenke, Inc., which is a source of income to Ms. Jenke.
Under the provisions of 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702(b)(3)(A), if:

       (A)  The effect of the decision will be to directly
increase or decrease the amount of income (other than
rents) to be received by the official, or to confer a
financial benefit or detriment upon the official or a
member of the official's immediate family, in an amount
of one hundred dollars ($100) or more;

then the decision will have a material financial effect on the
source of income and disqualification is required. Consequently,

_____________________
7/ See the following advice letters: Minor, No. A-77-069,
copy enclosed; Settleme, No. A-76-102, copy enclosed; and
telephone conversation memorandum T-76-107.

8/ As indicated previously, our advice is confined to the
provisions of the Political Reform Act and does not deal with
other conflicts of interest issues which may be raised under other
provisions of law. Counsel for West Side should be consulted on
these other issues, specifically Section 1090.

Jenke, et al.
February 22, 1983
Page 9


Mrs. Jenke will be disqualified from virtually every decision
having a non-salary, monetary effect on Jenke, Inc.

Dr. Arthur Griffin

Dr. Griffin has a financial interest in his medical
practice, Griffin, Inc. ("Griffin, Inc."). He has an
investment in excess of $1,000, receives income in excess of
$250 and is an officer and employee of Griffin, Inc. Section
87103, Dr. Griffin receives no salary from the Hospital or the
Nursing Home. Dr. Griffin also has a financial interest in
Jenke, Inc. and in fellow Director C. Ray Moore and the TMCL.
Each pays Griffin, Inc. more than $500 annually in rent.
Dr. Griffin also has a financial interest in the Medical Arts
Building.

Because Jenke, Inc. is a source of income to Dr. Griffin, he
may not participate in any decisions which will have a material
financial effect on Jenke, Inc. The appropriate guideline to be
applied in this instance is that found in 2 Cal. Adm. Code
Section 18702(b)(3)(C), which refers us, in turn, to subdivision
(D)(1) of that regulation, (copy enclosed).

Dr. Griffin will be subject to the cited guidelines regard-
less of whether the decision involves Dr. Jenke's salary or some
other economic impact on Jenke, Inc. Consequently, with respect
to any decisions affecting Jenke, Inc., Dr. Griffin will be
required to disqualify himself under any of the following
circumstances:

1. Where the reasonably foreseeable effect of the decision
on Jenke, Inc. will be to increase or decrease its annualized
gross revenues by $100,000 or more.

2. Where the reasonably foreseeable effect of the decision
on Jenke, Inc. will be to increase or decrease its annualized
gross revenues by $1,000 to $100,000 and the amount of the
increase or decrease represents 1% or more of Jenke, Inc.'s
annualized gross revenues.

3. Where the reasonably foreseeable effect of the decision
on Jenke, Inc. will be to increase or decrease its annual net
income by $50,000 or more.

4. Where the reasonably foreseeable effect of the decision
on Jenke, Inc. will be to increase or decrease its annual net
income by $1,000 to $50,000 and the amount of the increase or
decrease represents 1/2% or more of Jenke, Inc.'s annual net income.

Jenke, et al.
February 22, 1983
Page 10


     5.  Where the reasonably foreseeable effect of the decision
on Jenke, Inc. will be to increase or decrease the current
assets or liabilities of Jenke, Inc. by $100,000 or more. (See,
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702(b)(1), supra, for guidance on
what is included in current assets.)

     6.  Where the reasonably foreseeable effect of the decision
on Jenke, Inc. will be to increase or decrease the current
assets or liabilities of Jenke, Inc. by $1,000 to $100,000 and
the amount of the increase or decrease is 1/2% or more of the
value of the assets or liabilities.

     The same test would be applied with respect to decisions by
Dr. Griffin affecting C. Ray Moore and his business, TMCL. Even
if TMCL is not incorporated, a sole proprietorship or a
partnership is treated as a business entity for purposes of the
Act and the provisions of 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702.

     Dr. Griffin's income from seeing patients at the Hospital is
not salary (see Jenke analysis, supra); therefore, Dr. Griffin
will not be able to participate in decisions which directly
affect Griffin, Inc. if the effect is $100 or more (see, 2 Cal.
Adm. Code Section 18702(b)(3)(A), supra.) Thus, decisions as to
Dr. Griffin's privileges at the Hospital would almost assuredly
require his disqualification. The same would be true of any
decisions involving the contemplated litigation between
Dr. Griffin and West Side.

                              C. Ray Moore

     Mr. Moore has a financial interest in TMCL, both by reason
of his investment and his income. Dr. Griffin may be a source
of income to Mr. Moore in excess of $250 per year as a result of
payments for some lab work (where the "no charge" services would
otherwise exceed the contractual limit).

     Aside from Dr. Griffin, very few of the physicians in Taft
make direct payments to Mr. Moore or TMCL. Only those which do
make direct payments to TMCL totaling $500 or more in the
preceding 12-month period would constitute a source of income to
Mr. Moore and constitute a financial interest for Mr. Moore.9/

_____
     9/ Since Mr. Moore owns TMCL together with his wife, the
income to TMCL is pro-rated one-half to each. Hence, $500 in
gross receipts to TMCL will constitute $250 in pro rata income
to Mr. Moore. This test applies to Dr. Griffin as well.

.

Jenke, et al.
February 22, 1983
Page 11


     Mr. Moore will be required to disqualify himself from
participating in any decision which will have a material
financial effect on TMCL (guideline 18702(b)(3)(A) applies) or
on Griffin, Inc. or any other physician's practice meets the
threshold described above (gudeline 18702(b)(1) applies).  Thus,
as to decisions regarding referrals by the Hospital's lab to
TMCL for certain tests, Mr. Moore must disqualify himself if the
reasonably foreseeable effect on TMCL will be $100 or more.10/
As to decisions affecting paying physicians, each will have to be
examined as to its relative impact on the physician, applying the
guidelines cited above.  This may include Dr. Griffin and may
include Dr. Jenke (if either makes sufficient payments).

                              Conclusion

     Each of you will encounter numerous and various circum-
stances where you must disqualify from participation.  In the
event that three or more West Side Directors are required to
disqualify as to any particular decision, you should follow the
procedures set forth in the Commission's Hudson Opinion (4 FPPC
Opinions 13, No. 77-007, February 7, 1978), a copy of which is
enclosed.  We also enclose a copy of our pamphlet explaining the
conflict of interest provisions of the Act.

     If you have further questions, please contact me at
916/322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Robert E. Leidigh
                                        Counsel
                                        Legal Division

REL:km
Enclosures




_______________________________
  10/  As to any such decision, Mr. Moore should also consult
with West Side's counsel regarding other provisions of the law.

performed unless a physician has prescribed that worK. 
Most 
work performed by TMCL is billed directly to the patient (or 
insurance carrier where applicable), not to the physicians; 
however, some work is billed to the physician. 
Jenke, Inc. and 
Dr. Griffin both regularly refer clients to TMCL for lab work. 
The Hospital also has a lab of its own, which performs some 
services in competition with TMCL. 
Mr. Moore has received some 
information to the effect that some doctors are required by the 
Hospital to use its lab ratner than TMCL. 
Mr. Moore would like 
to have the Hospital lab refer tests to his lab (TMCL) which are 
currently being referred to labs outside the community. 
TMCL 
performs these tests which no other lab in the community 
performs.
sections.analysis:
The analysis will consider each of you, in turn, both 
generally and with respect to the two specific issues coming up 
on February 23rd. 
First, the general principals of the Act 
regarding conflicts of interest will be set forth. 


Jenke, et al. 
FebruarY-22; 1983  
The Act prohibits a public official from making, partici-
pating in making, or using his or her official position to 
influence the making of, a decision in which he or she knows or 
has reason to know that he or she has a financial interest. 
Section 87100. 
A financial interest is defined in Section 87103 
as follows: 
An official has a financial interest in a decision 
witnin the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision will have a material 
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on 
the public generally, on: 
(a) 
Any business entity in which the puolic 
official has a direct or indirect investment worth 
more than one thousand dollars ($1,000). 
(b) 
Any real property in which the pUblic 
official has a direct or indirect investment worth 
more than one thousand dollars ($1,000). 
(C) 
Any source of income, other than loans 
by a commercial lending institution in the regular 
course of business on terms available to the 
public without regard to official status, aggre-
gating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in 
value provided to, received by or promised to the 
public official within 12 months prior to the time 
when the decision is made. 
(d) 
Any business entity in which the public 
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, 
employee, or holds any position of management. 
For purposes of this section, indirect investment 
or interest means any investment or interest owned by 
the spouse or dependent child of a public official, by 
an agent on behalf of a public official, or by a 
business entity or trust in which tne official, the 
official's agents, spouse, and dependent children own 
directly, or beneficially a 10 percent interest or 
greater. 
With these basic principles in mind we turn now to their 
application to the facts at hand. 


Jenke, et ale 
FeOruarY-22; 1983  
Mrs. Barbara Jenke 
Mrs. Jenke has a financial interest in Jenke, Inc., which is 
a source of income to her of more than $250 (Section 87103(c». 
She also has an investment of more than $1,000 in Jenke, Inc. 
(Section 87103(a» 
and serves as both an officer and an employee 
of Jenke, Inc. (Section 87103(d». 
Jenke, Inc. receives money from West Side. 
We must 
determine whether this money constitutes income within the 
meaning of the Act in order to know whether West Side may be 
considered a source of income to Mrs. Jenke, which could require 
her to disqualify herself as to most decisions affecting West 
Side. 
Income is defined in Section 82030(a) as follows: 
(a) 
"Income" means, except as provided in sub-
section (b), a payment received, including but not 
limited to any salary, wage, advance, dividend, 
interest, rent proceeds from any sale, gift, including 
any gift of food or beverage, loan, forgiveness or 
payment of indebtedness received by the filer, 
reimbursement for expenses, per diem, or contrioution 
to an insurance or pension program paid by any person 
other than an employer, and including any community 
property interest in income of a spouse. 
Income also 
includes an outstanding loan. 
Income of an individual 
also includes a pro rata share of any income of any 
business entity or trust in which the individual or 
spouse owns, directly, indirectly or beneficially, a 
10-percent interest or greater. 
"Income," other than a 
gift, does not include income received from any source 
outside the jurisdiction and not doing business within 
the jurisdiction, not planning to do business within 
the jurisdiction, or not having done business within 
the jurisdiction during the two years prior to the time 
any statement or other action is required under this 
title. 
Section 82030(b) excludes certain things from the definition of 
income which would otherwise be covered by Section 82030 (a). 
In 
pertinent part, Section 82030(b) reads as follows: 
(b) 
"Income" also does not include:

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Each of you has asked for advice as to your respective 
duties regarding possible disqualification under the Act in 
light of the foregoing facts. 
In particular, the West Side 
Board will De taking the following actions at its next meeting, 
Feoruary 23, 1983: 
1. 
The Board will review and approve or disapprove checks 
drawn to pay Dr. JenKe for his services as Medical Director at 
West Side's Nursing Home and his interpretation work at the 
Hospital. 
This is normally a routine matter and these checks 
have never been disapproved. 
2. 
The Board will consider the extension of various staff 
privileges to both Dr. Jenke and Dr. Griffin.

CONCLUSION: Each of you will encounter numerous and various circum-
stances where you must disqualify from participation. 
In the 
event that three or more West Side Directors are required to 
disqualify as to any particular decision, you snould follow the 
procedures set forth in the Commission's Hudson Opinion (4 FPPC 
Opinions 13, No. 77-007, February 7, 1978), a copy of which is 
enclosed. 
We also enclose a copy of our pamphlet explaining the 
conflict of interest provisions of the Act.

BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest (916) 322-5662 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441 322-6444 February 22, 1983 Mrs. Barbara Jenke Arthur Griffin, M.D. C. Ray Moore Members of the Board of Directors Westside Hospital District c/o James Jenke, M.D., Inc. 109 Adkisson Way Taft, CA 93268 Re: Your Mutual Requests for Written Advice, Our File No. A-83-001 Dear Directors: Each of you has separately requested that we provide written advice to you regarding your obligations under the Political Form Act/ (the "Act"). Because the facts of your situa- tions are so intricately intertwined, we have chosen to respond to you in one, combined advice letter. The facts are complex and will be stated below. FACTS Each of you was recently elected to the West Side Hospital District Board of Directors. Each of you commenced your respective term of office on Janauary 6, 1983. The West Side Hospital District ("West Side") is a public entity and a local governmental agency within the meaning of the Act. It is organized pursuant to Division 23 of the Health and Safety Code (commencing with Section 32000) known as "The Local Hospital District Law". The Board of Directors is publicly elected pursuant to Health and Safety Code Section 32100. _________________________ 1/ Government Code Sections 81001-91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise specified. Our advice is limited solely to issues raised under the Act. Questions involving other provisions of law, such as Section 1090, should be addressed to other counsel, such as that for the West Side Hospital District. Jenke, et al. February 22, 1983 Page 2 Mrs. Barbara Jenke Mrs. Jenke is a director of West Side. She is the spouse of James Jenke, M.D., who has a medical practice in Taft. She serves as Dr. Jenke's office manager. Dr. Jenke's practice is incorporated (James Jenke, M.D., Inc.) and Mrs. Jenke is an officer in that business entity (Secretary-Treasurer). Dr. Jenke is the sole shareholder in the corporation.2/ He draws a salary as does Mrs. Jenke. Profits are distributed to Dr. Jenke through an "end-of-year bonus". Mrs. Jenke has a community property interest in one-half of Dr. Jenke's earnings. Dr. Jenke's practice ("Jenke, Inc.") receives income from West Side's Nursing Home in the sum of $750 per month as its Medical Director. In addition, Dr. Jenke receives income from the patients for seeing them at the Nursing Home. Dr. Jenke also is referred patients by the West Side District Hospital ("Hospital") for interpretations of various tests, including EKGs.3/ Dr. Jenke also currently serves, without compensa- tion, as both Chief of Medical Staff for the Hospital and as the Medical Director for the Hospital's Cardiopulmonary Laboratory. Jenke, Inc. rents its medical office space in the Medical Arts Building from Dr. Arthur Griffin, M.D., Inc. Dr. Griffin is also a member of the West
